quote: My name is Brandon. I'm here with Solar Energy.
stewart_read: The rep introduces himself as being with 'Solar Energy' — not ION Solar, not the Tesla-certified installer framing the codex specifies. The intro beat's primary job is legitimacy: answering 'is this a scam?' in the first 30 seconds. 'Solar Energy' is a generic phrase that does the opposite — it sounds like every door-knocker and robocall the customer has ever received. Strategic hypothesis: the rep is deliberately softening the company name to reduce resistance. Reactive hypothesis: the rep simply failed to execute the intro. The reactive read holds — there is no evidence of a deliberate strategy, and the rest of the call shows no compensating legitimacy move.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep deliberately used a softer company reference to reduce early resistance before establishing ION's identity.
coaching_implication: Pull this clip in the 1-on-1. Ask Brandon to say the intro correctly from memory. The Tesla-certified framing is not optional decoration — it is the legitimacy anchor that makes everything else easier. If he cannot recite it cold, he needs reps before the next shift.

stewart_read: The rep phrases the time anchor as a question with 'around' — 'Around 12:00 work for you?' This is a soft time anchor, not a locked one. The codex distinguishes callbacks with locked times from 'we'll touch base' arrangements on no-show rate. 'Around 12:00' sits closer to the latter. The customer confirms 'Monday at twelve' in the rep's follow-up restatement, which partially recovers the lock, but the rep's own phrasing introduced ambiguity first.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep used a soft phrasing deliberately to reduce friction with a customer who had already said 'maybe' — a low-resistance close.
coaching_implication: Show Brandon the contrast: 'Around 12:00 work for you?' vs. 'I've got you at 12:00 on Monday the thirteenth — does that work?' The second version locks the time without adding friction. The 'around' hedge is a habit that compounds across a pay period in no-shows.

quote: So it looks like we have an evaluation for your home. Right? We just don't have an electric bill.
stewart_read: This is the rep's only qualification move in the entire call, and it is not a verify item — it is a bill-collection prompt. No address confirmation, no homeowner check, no title/co-owner question, no interest anchor, no utility company, no roof age. The verify section requires 6 of 8 items for a qualified appointment. Zero were completed. The rep is booking a sit on a customer whose qualification status is entirely unknown.
alternative_hypothesis: This is a recycled lead with existing records on file, and the rep correctly skipped verify items already captured. The codex notes that short calls may legitimately compress beats if existing records cover qualification.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis is the coaching question to ask Brandon directly: 'Did you have this customer's records in front of you? What did you know about him before the call?' If he had records, the skip was defensible. If he didn't, this is a full verify miss — and the manager needs to know whether Brandon understands the difference between a recycled lead and a cold one.

quote: We just don't have an electric bill.
stewart_read: The rep identifies the missing bill and defers collection to the appointment. The codex's bill_collection section distinguishes by lead type: paid leads require the bill before the appointment can be set; organic leads prefer it but do not require it. There is no evidence in the transcript that Brandon made a conscious lead-type determination. If this is a paid lead, deferring the bill is a protocol violation that puts the sit at risk. If this is an organic or recycled lead, deferral is permissible. The gap is not purely reactive behavior — it may reflect lead-type rule ignorance rather than willful avoidance — but the outcome is the same: the closer walks in without the bill.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep correctly identified this as an organic or recycled lead and appropriately deferred bill collection to the specialist.
coaching_implication: The manager needs to pull the lead-type metadata for this call. If it is a paid lead, Brandon needs to understand that deferring the bill is not a scheduling convenience — it is a protocol violation that risks the sit not counting. If it is organic, the coaching shifts to efficiency: could he have captured the bill in the 90 seconds the customer said 'it's probably in my emails'?

quote: I it's probably in my emails. Do you guys just need the graph Yes. Of black monthly something?
stewart_read: The customer volunteers that the bill is in his emails and demonstrates prior familiarity with the kWh graph — 'the graph, yes, of black monthly something.' This is a customer who has engaged with his bill before, likely in a prior solar conversation. He is not starting from zero. The rep acknowledges the graph but does not use this moment to anchor bill pain or create curiosity about what the numbers mean for his specific home. The enthusiasm signal — a customer who already knows what the graph is — is walked past entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep correctly kept scope narrow and deferred the bill-pain conversation to the specialist, consistent with setter-scope discipline.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis has merit — the setter's job is not to sell solar. But there is a middle path: 'You already know the graph — that's actually helpful. When our specialist pulls that up Monday, he'll show you exactly what those numbers translate to in savings for your home.' That line creates curiosity without scope creep. Show Brandon the difference between deferring the conversation and walking past a warm signal entirely.

quote: Just just that first page that we need. Thanks, Michael. I'll see you, this Monday at 12:00.
stewart_read: The rep closes the call without completing any of the required button-up elements: no one-hour block stated, no phone appointment specified, no 'not driving, at a computer' anchor, no 'is that fair' verbal handshake, no text follow-up promise. The codex marks several of these as required and ties the 'is that fair' handshake directly to show rate. Additionally, the rep addresses the customer as 'Michael' — the customer's name has not appeared in the transcript prior to this moment, suggesting it came from a record, but the rep never used it during the call to build rapport. The button-up is a logistics sign-off, not an appointment confirmation.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep kept the call short deliberately to avoid over-talking a cooperative customer who had already agreed to the appointment.
coaching_implication: The button-up is not about length — it is about anchoring the commitment. 'Is that fair?' takes four seconds. 'You'll want to be at your computer, not driving' takes five. These are not rapport-building extras; they are show-rate mechanics. Walk Brandon through each required element and ask him to add them to his close without adding more than 60 seconds to the call.

quote: Yes. Yes. Looks like, we weren't able to meet with you to go over that full evaluation, but I I have a specialist here in the area who can help explore a few options with you.
stewart_read: The rep's framing — 'a specialist here in the area' — implies an in-person visit, not a phone appointment. The codex's button_up section specifies that the appointment is a phone call, and the no-driving/at-computer anchor exists precisely because customers need to know it is a screen-share or phone review, not a home visit. The rep's intro framing may be creating a mismatched expectation that the closer will have to correct on Monday.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep used 'in the area' loosely as a legitimacy signal, not as a literal description of the appointment format.
coaching_implication: Ask Brandon: 'What does Michael think is happening Monday at noon — a phone call or someone coming to his house?' If Brandon isn't sure, that is the coaching gap. The button-up anchor ('you'll want to be at your computer') would have corrected this, which is another reason the skipped button-up elements compound each other.
